Title: Sub-cellular metal imaging identifies dynamic sites of Cu accumulation in Chlamydomonas

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Assimilation of Cu+ needed for cuproproteins
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Initially plasma membrane uptake into cell; excess then sequestered in acidocalcisome-like compartments
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Abundant in Zn-limited cells; rapidly decreased during first 3–5 h after Zn resupply
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Upregulated in Zn limitation despite adequate extracellular Cu and high intracellular Cu; reduced after Zn resupply
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Drive Cu+ hyperaccumulation under Zn limitation by increasing uptake
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq; qRT-PCR inference via Cu regulon activity; mutant/complementation with CRR1; ICP-MS
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can cause excessive intracellular Cu accumulation when Cu is biologically inaccessible
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential targets to modulate Cu uptake, though overactivation risks toxic or mislocalized Cu accumulation
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Three CTR genes were monitored together in transcriptome analysis; specific identities not detailed in provided text
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CTR transcripts are abundant in Zn-limited cells. 2. High CTR expression likely drives Cu hyperaccumulation. 3. Their transcript levels rapidly fall after Zn resupply as Cu homeostasis is restored.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Indicator of Cu nutritional status affecting photosynthetic electron carrier substitution
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Strongly induced in Zn-limited cells and absent in Cu- and Zn-replete cells; rapidly decreased after Zn resupply
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts as a sentinel marker showing biological Cu deficiency under Zn limitation
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful biomarker of intracellular Cu deficiency rather than direct engineering target in this paper
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not causal for Cu storage itself but central experimental readout of CRR1-mediated Cu deficiency response
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Zn limitation; Zn resupply; Cu deficiency
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYC6 transcripts and protein accumulate strongly in Zn-limited cells. 2. This reveals biological Cu deficiency despite total Cu hyperaccumulation. 3. Its rapid decline after Zn resupply indicates restoration of Cu visibility and homeostasis.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Intracellular Cu delivery for cuproprotein biosynthesis
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Mobilized from acidocalcisome-like compartments to chloroplast/secretory pathway destinations
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Transcripts increased soon after Zn resupply
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced after Zn resupply when Cu is redistributed
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely facilitates redistribution of mobilized Cu+ to cuproprotein assembly pathways after Zn resupply
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq correlation with Cu mobilization and plastocyanin synthesis
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to improve intracellular Cu allocation efficiency for cuproprotein assembly
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed as handling component rather than directly functionally manipulated
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ATX1 transcript abundance rises soon after Zn resupply. 2. This timing coincides with Cu mobilization from storage compartments. 3. ATX1 is proposed to aid redistribution of released Cu for plastocyanin and other cuproproteins.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Intracellular Cu delivery, especially toward chloroplast/plastocyanin pathway
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Mobilized from acidocalcisome-like compartments to chloroplast destinations
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Transcripts increased soon after Zn resupply
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with Cu redistribution to plastocyanin biosynthesis after Zn resupply
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq correlation with isotope-traced plastocyanin Cu loading
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential engineering target for improving Cu delivery to chloroplast cuproproteins
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Named in text as Cu+ chaperone involved in Cu handling during restored homeostasis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PCC1 transcripts increase shortly after Zn resupply. 2. The induction parallels increased Cu bioavailability and plastocyanin synthesis. 3. PCC1 is implicated in productive intracellular redistribution of mobilized Cu.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Intracellular distribution of Cu against concentration gradients
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Secretory pathway and chloroplast-directed Cu trafficking
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Transcripts increased soon after Zn resupply
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Upregulated during restoration of Cu homeostasis after Zn resupply
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely support redistribution of released Cu to plastocyanin biosynthesis and iron uptake machinery
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate targets to improve intracellular Cu partitioning without increasing external Cu inputs
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Specific transporter names were not provided in the supplied text
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Intracellular Cu-transporting ATPase transcripts increase after Zn resupply. 2. Their induction matches the period of Cu remobilization. 3. They are proposed to help route released Cu to productive destinations.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Binds and incorporates Cu+ into plastocyanin to form the functional cuproprotein used in photosynthetic electron transport
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Required cofactor for plastocyanin and photosynthetic electron transport
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Acidocalcisome-like Cu-accumulating compartments and chloroplast plastocyanin pool
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Transcript abundance increased after Zn resupply
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Low under Zn limitation; strongly increased upon Zn resupply concomitant with Cu mobilization
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Plastocyanin accumulation is strongly stimulated after Zn resupply using mobilized intracellular Cu+
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Immunoblotting; LC-ICP-MS Cu isotope labeling; RNA-seq
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Zn limitation followed by Zn resupply; Cu isotope labeling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Limited direct crop application; useful marker for intracellular Cu bioavailability and cuproprotein metallation efficiency
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although plastocyanin itself is a cuproprotein rather than a classical metabolic enzyme, it was a central experimentally tracked Cu-dependent protein demonstrating mobilization of stored Cu
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PCY1 transcripts increased after Zn resupply. 2. Newly synthesized plastocyanin preferentially incorporated Cu from the intracellular sequestered pool. 3. Plastocyanin accumulation served as direct evidence that stored Cu became bioavailable.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Essential micronutrient for cuproprotein biosynthesis including plastocyanin and iron uptake components
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Functionally active in Cu nutritional signaling; target genes induced in Zn-limited cells and rapidly deactivated after Zn resupply, then later reactivated as Cu became depleted during growth
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Zn limitation; Zn resupply; Cu deficiency
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Cellular Cu status; Zn limitation indirectly causes biological Cu deficiency and CRR1 activation; Zn resupply restores Cu visibility
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates nutritional Cu regulon including CTR genes and CYC6 under biological Cu deficiency; deactivated rapidly after Zn resupply as mobilized Cu becomes visible
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation responsive to Cu nutritional status
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Feedback homeostasis: active when Cu is biologically unavailable, repressed when mobilized Cu becomes visible, later reactivated as intracellular Cu is consumed
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential controller of Cu hyperaccumulation under Zn limitation; crr1 mutants fail to hyperaccumulate Cu
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes Cu hyperaccumulation during Zn limitation by activating Cu uptake regulon despite high total intracellular Cu
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (crr1-2); complementation; qRT-PCR; RNA-seq; ICP-MS; confocal imaging
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Sustained activation under Zn limitation leads to biologically inaccessible Cu hyperaccumulation
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential leverage point for engineering Cu nutrient sensing and controlled metal storage/mobilization, but manipulation may risk metal mis-homeostasis
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Sequestered Cu is invisible to CRR1; Zn deficiency creates biological but not chemical Cu deficiency. Discussion notes SBP domain binds Cu+ with much higher affinity than Zn2+
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CRR1 is required for Zn-deficiency-induced Cu hyperaccumulation. 2. CRR1 activates CTR transporter expression and the Cu regulon when Cu is biologically unavailable. 3. Upon Zn resupply, mobilized Cu becomes visible to CRR1, causing rapid deactivation of the regulon.
